title: Personal Guarantors & Workmen's Dues
content:
Session 9: Personal guarantors, surety and workmen dues 
Lalit Kumar v Union of India  [2021 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Multiple writ petitions across High Courts challenged the MCA Notification dated 15.11.2019, which was then transferred 
to the Supreme Court under Article 32.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The notification operationalised Part III of the IBC only for personal guarantors to corporate debtors. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The petitioners were individuals who had given personal guarantees for loans taken by companies they were connected with.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč After these companies entered CIRP, creditors invoked the guarantees and issued demand notices proposing Part III 
proceedings before the NCLT. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Contended that the notification exceeded Section 1(3), amounted to impermissible selective enforcement of Part III, violated 
Article 14 by isolating personal guarantors from the larger class of individuals, and was ultra vires because Part III is 
inseverable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč They argued that Section 1(3) allows phased enforcement but not selective application to subclasses, that only the DRT can 
adjudicate individual insolvency under Section 179, and that approval of a corporate debtor‚Äôs resolution plan under Section 
31 discharges personal guarantors. 
 
Issues: Whether the 15.11.2019 MCA Notification, which brought into force selected provisions of Part III of the IBC only insofar 
as they apply to personal guarantors of corporate debtors, was: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč An instance of excessive delegation and ultra vires Section 1(3) of the IBC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arbitrary and unconstitutional under Article 14 due to selective application only against personal guarantors. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Contrary to the scheme of the IBC, which allegedly does not contemplate initiating Part III proceedings solely against 
personal guarantors while excluding other individuals. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Invalid because the Code does not permit personal guarantors to be treated differently or tried before the NCLT. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Invalid because approval of a resolution plan under Part II automatically discharges guarantors. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 1(3) of the IBC ‚Äď empowers the Central Government to notify different provisions of the Code on different dates. 
The proviso permits bringing any provision or part thereof into force at different times. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 2(e), (f), (g) (post-2018 amendment) ‚Äď creates separate categories: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(e): personal guarantors to corporate debtors (distinct category). 
66 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(f): partnership firms and partners. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 2(g): individuals. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 60 ‚Äď  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Designates NCLT as the adjudicating authority for: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Corporate debtors, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Corporate guarantors, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Personal guarantors to corporate debtors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(2) mandates that if CIRP of a corporate debtor is pending before NCLT, insolvency/bankruptcy of its 
personal guarantor must also be before the same NCLT. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(3) transfers pending proceedings against personal guarantors from DRT to NCLT. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(4) grants NCLT all powers of DRT for Part III. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 179 ‚Äď DRT is adjudicating authority for individuals, but subject to Section 60. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 238 ‚Äď IBC overrides all inconsistent laws. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Sections 234‚Äď235 ‚Äď enable cross-border insolvency coordination for corporate debtors and their personal guarantors, 
reflecting identical treatment for asset realisation. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On excessive delegation and vires of the notification 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 1(3) allows the CG to operationalise provisions of the IBC in stages ‚Üí the provision‚Äôs language and 
historical implementation (previous staggered notifications) demonstrate legislative intent for phased enforcement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The 2018 Amendment subdivided individuals into three distinct subcategories ‚Üí a deliberate legislative act 
enabling targeted enforcement for specific groups. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Personal guarantors are uniquely and intrinsically connected to corporate debtors ‚Üí intentionally treated 
them as a separate species. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
EARLIER, all individuals were under one class, making selective enforcement difficult ‚áí 
post-amendment differentiation provides a statutory basis for isolating personal guarantors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the notification is consistent with the statutory scheme and within the government‚Äôs delegated authority 
under Section 1(3). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On constitutional challenge under Article 14 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Differential treatment is permissible IF intelligible differentia and rational nexus exist ‚áí notification furthers the 
object of the Code ‚Üí maximising value and ensuring coherent insolvency processes. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
HERE, personal guarantors are closely linked with CD, unlike other individuals. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Common adjudication before NCLT ensures consistency, avoids conflicting outcomes, and allows CoC a 
comprehensive picture of available assets. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Parallel processes in different forums (DRT for guarantor, NCLT for corporate debtor) would lead to 
inefficiency and uncertainty. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí the classification is reasonable, non-arbitrary, and has direct nexus with legislative purpose ‚áí no Article 14 
violation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On selective enforcement of Part III only for personal guarantors 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Petitioners argued that provisions of Part III are inseverable and must be applied to all natural persons 
simultaneously. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Court rejected this: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Part III‚Äôs provisions can be applied to different categories at different times. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 2(e) creates a distinct subgroup. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Parliament itself intended personal guarantors to be treated differently. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLT‚Äôs jurisdiction over guarantors flows directly from Section 60(2), independent of full enforcement 
of Part III for other individuals. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On alleged inconsistency between Part II and Part III 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Petitioners argued that applying Part III processes before NCLT creates procedural anomalies. 
67 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Court held no incongruity arises because: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 60(4) expressly grants NCLT DRT‚Äôs powers for proceedings against personal guarantors under 
Part III. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Code merges adjudication for guarantors with corporate debtors deliberately for efficiency. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 179 expressly operates ‚Äúsubject to Section 60‚ÄĚ. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč On argument that resolution plan approval discharges personal guarantor liability ‚Üí reaffirmed that a contract of 
guarantee is an independent contract. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Approval of a resolution plan does not ipso facto discharge guarantors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The guarantor‚Äôs liability arises from the guarantee contract, not from the corporate debtor‚Äôs insolvency. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This holds consistent with earlier cases such as SBI v Ramakrishnan. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On legislative intent from scheme of Code 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Sections 234-235 show similar treatment of overseas assets of corporate debtors and guarantors. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Non-notification of Section 243 (which would repeal earlier guarantor laws) was deliberate to ensure proceedings 
against guarantors continue. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The overall scheme shows consistent intent to have personal guarantors‚Äô proceedings tied to corporate debtors‚Äô 
proceedings. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Notification is purely operational and facilitates pending actions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No excessive delegation; no colourable exercise of power. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Personal guarantors are legitimately treated as a distinct class. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Notification harmonises with Section 2(e), Section 60, and overall IBC scheme. 
 
Dilip Jiwrajka v Union of India [2023 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Part III governs insolvency resolution and bankruptcy for individuals and partnership firms. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Sections 94‚Äď120 create the IRP for individuals. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 79(1) makes the DRT the adjudicating authority for Part III. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under Section 97, upon a Section 94/95 application, the adjudicating authority appoints a RP. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 99 mandates the RP to examine the application, seek relevant information, and file a reasoned 
recommendatory report suggesting approval or rejection. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 100 provides for the judicial determination of the application by the DRT after the RP‚Äôs report. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 96 imposes an interim moratorium from the filing of the application until admission; Section 101 imposes a 
moratorium after admission. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Petitioners challenged Sections 95‚Äď100, arguing they violate natural justice and Articles 14 & 21 because an RP is appointed 
and an automatic moratorium is triggered before any judicial determination of default. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Sections 95‚Äď100 of the IBC violate Articles 14 and 21 due to: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč automatic interim moratorium (Section 96), 
‚ěĘ‚Äč appointment and actions of the Resolution Professional without prior adjudication, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč alleged absence of hearing at the pre-Section 100 stage. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the RP under Section 99 performs an unconstitutional adjudicatory role. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Part II (Corporate Insolvency) vs Part III (Individual Insolvency): 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Part II: adjudicating authority plays an adjudicatory role at the threshold; IRP takes over management; 
moratorium declared under Section 14 after judicial determination. 
68 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Part III: RP appointed early as a facilitator, not adjudicator; no takeover of assets/business; moratorium under 
Section 96 is protective, triggered automatically to prevent harassment. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 99:  
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP must: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
examine the application, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
collect information ‚Äúrelatable to the application‚ÄĚ, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
submit a recommendatory, non-binding report. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP cannot make binding factual findings; RP owes confidentiality (Regulation 7(2)(h) of IP Regulations 2016). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 100: Adjudicating authority must decide on admission, and audi alteram partem is read into this section even if 
silent. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Role of RP is Facilitatory, Not Adjudicatory 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Expressions in Section 99 such as ‚Äúexamine‚ÄĚ, ‚Äúascertain‚ÄĚ, ‚Äúsatisfies the requirements‚ÄĚ indicate a non-adjudicatory, 
administrative function. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The report is recommendatory; AA does not treat it as binding. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP cannot take over assets, management or make judicial determinations (unlike Part II). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Justification for Different Treatment Between Part II and Part III 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Individual defaults can be as low as ‚āĻ1,000; requiring judicial adjudication of every such default at the threshold 
would overwhelm tribunals. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Legislature intentionally created an intermediate filtering stage via RP to collate material for the adjudicating 
authority. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, intelligible differentia exists between corporate and individual insolvency processes. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Moratorium Under Section 96 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Unlike Section 14, Section 96 moratorium is protective ‚Üí meant to shield debtors/guarantors from legal 
harassment while facts are compiled. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Restrictions on asset alienation arise only after admission under Section 101. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Natural Justice 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No violation of natural justice: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RP‚Äôs enquiries are limited and cannot be a roving investigation. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 99(4) explicitly grants the creditor a hearing. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 100 must be interpreted to read in a hearing requirement for the debtor (audi alteram partem). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RP is bound by confidentiality and cannot prejudge issues. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The RP‚Äôs actions do not cause ‚Äúcivil consequences‚ÄĚ requiring pre-decision hearing. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Constitutionality Under Article 14 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The structure [RP‚Äôs limited role, early-stage moratorium, final adjudication at Section 100] has rational nexus 
with the object of individual insolvency resolution ‚Üí neither arbitrary nor excessive. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Sections 95 to 100 of the IBC are constitutionally valid. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RP‚Äôs role is administrative and recommendatory, not adjudicatory. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No violation of Articles 14 or 21 occurs because: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč the moratorium under Section 96 is protective, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč natural justice is preserved through Section 99(4) and the reading-in of a hearing at Section 100, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč adjudication happens only at Section 100 by a judicial authority. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Thus, the constitutional challenge fails; Part III as structured is upheld. 
 
BRS Ventures Investments Limited v SREI Infrastructure Finance Limited [2024 | SC | 2 J] 
 
69 
 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD had availed a loan of ‚āĻ100 crore from the FC ‚Üí secured through a corporate guarantee issued by ACIL, the holding 
company of the CD ‚Üí default, guarantor did not perform ‚Üí initiated CIRP against the guarantor (ACIL) under Part II of 
the IBC (because it was a corporate guarantee). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A Resolution Plan for the guarantor was approved, under which the FC accepted a haircut. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč For the remaining unrecovered amount, the FC proceeded against the CD. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Argument of the guarantor/CD: once the FC has recovered partly from the guarantor and the guarantor‚Äôs CIRP has been 
resolved, only the guarantor can recover any remaining amount from the CD (via subrogation). The FC itself cannot 
proceed against the CD for the remaining outstanding. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč What is the liability of a corporate guarantor under the Indian Contract Act and IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does approval of a Resolution Plan for a corporate guarantor extinguish the creditor‚Äôs right to proceed against the principal 
borrower for the remaining debt? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can simultaneous/parallel proceedings lie against the corporate debtor and corporate guarantor under Section 60? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether approval of the guarantor‚Äôs Resolution Plan extinguishes the CD‚Äôs liability or the FC‚Äôs right to recover from the 
CD? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Extent of a creditor‚Äôs right of subrogation under Section 140 of the Contract Act. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 128, Indian Contract Act ‚Äď Co-extensive Liability 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Liability of surety = liability of principal debtor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Creditors may proceed against the debtor, the guarantor, or both simultaneously, without exhausting remedies 
against either. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Settlements / Part Payments 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Part satisfaction from the guarantor does not extinguish the balance liability of the debtor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The creditor retains the right to recover the balance from the principal borrower. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even if the guarantor and creditor settle, the debtor remains liable unless expressly discharged. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC Principles 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Approval of a Resolution Plan of the principal borrower does not discharge the personal guarantor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Conversely, approval of a Resolution Plan of the guarantor does not discharge the principal borrower, because the 
guarantee contract is independent. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(2)‚Äď(3) IBC 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Permits simultaneous or independent CIRP proceedings against CD and corporate guarantor. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Provides for transfer to the same bench where both proceedings are pending. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Subrogation (Section 140, Contract Act) ‚Äď a surety who pays the debt gets rights of the creditor only to the extent of the 
amount paid, not beyond. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Liability of Guarantor is Co-extensive 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The guarantor‚Äôs argument that only it (after payment) can recover from the CD is contrary to Section 128 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Creditors may proceed first against the guarantor. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Or first against the CD. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Or both simultaneously. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No requirement to exhaust remedies against CD before guarantor. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Effect of Resolution Plan of the Guarantor 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Through the CIRP, the guarantor‚Äôs liability was discharged by operation of law (IBC), not by voluntary contract 
‚Üí does NOT extinguish the CD‚Äôs liability. 
70 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since the underlying debt is not fully satisfied, the FC retains the right to recover the remainder from the 
CD. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Discharge of debtor does not discharge guarantor. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Discharge of guarantor does not discharge debtors. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Both obligations are independent contracts. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Simultaneous Proceedings Under Section 60 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 60(2)‚Äď(3) explicitly permits parallel CIRPs against the CD and CG. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Hence, FC‚Äôs action against CD after CG‚Äôs CIRP is fully valid. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The purpose is to preserve co-extensive liability. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT Findings on the Resolution Plan 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Clauses 12.3 & 13.3 of the Resolution Plan did not demonstrate that FC had accepted a full and final settlement of 
all dues. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Thus, rights against CDs were not extinguished. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On Subrogation (Section 140) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The guarantor/CD argued that the FC cannot pursue CD because subrogation vests in the guarantor alone ‚Üí 
REJECTED 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Subrogation arises only to the extent of the amount paid by the guarantor ‚Üí here, the ‚Äúpayment‚ÄĚ 
occurred via a haircut-based resolution plan ‚Üí not full payment. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Therefore, FC retains the right to proceed against CD for the balance of unpaid debt. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Liability of guarantor and principal borrower is co-extensive ‚Äď FC can proceed against either or both, simultaneously or 
separately. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Approval of a Resolution Plan for the corporate guarantor does not extinguish the liability of the corporate debtor for the 
remaining debt. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Discharge of guarantor through CIRP (involuntary, via operation of law) does not discharge the CD. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No double recovery is permitted: CD is liable only to the extent of the remaining unpaid debt, after deducting what FC 
recovered in guarantor‚Äôs CIRP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Subrogation rights under Section 140 do not override creditor‚Äôs rights ‚Äď -hey arise only to the extent of the amount actually 
paid by the guarantor. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 60(2) permits simultaneous proceedings against CD and corporate guarantor; no bar to FC‚Äôs actions. 
 
Sunil Kumar Jain v. Sundaresh Bhatt, [2022 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD operated shipbuilding yards at Dahej, Surat, and offices in Mumbai, employing several hundred workmen and staff ‚Äď 
CIRP was initiated after admission of a Section 7 application. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No resolution plan succeeded; liquidation was ordered and R1 was appointed as liquidator. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Workmen sought payment of wages/salaries for the CIRP period and for some pre-CIRP periods ‚Üí the AA rejected these 
claims; the NCLAT affirmed, directing them to approach the liquidator. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Workmen argued that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Their wages for the CIRP period must be treated as CIRP costs under s.5(13) r/w s.53(1)(a). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Workmen dues should get priority as per Companies Act ss. 326‚Äď327. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Liquidator argued that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Only those who actually worked when CD was a going concern during CIRP can qualify under s.5(13); 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Workmen‚Äôs dues under PF, gratuity, pension are excluded from liquidation estate under s.36(4) IBC. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether wages/salaries of workmen during the CIRP period form part of ‚ÄúCIRP costs‚ÄĚ under s.5(13) and therefore must 
be paid first under s.53(1)(a)? What conditions must be satisfied to treat such dues as CIRP costs? 
71 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč How do workmen‚Äôs dues fit within the liquidation waterfall under s.53 IBC? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Specifically: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
What happens to pre-CIRP wages/salaries? 
‚Ė†‚Äč
What is the treatment of PF, pension, and gratuity funds? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether ss. 326‚Äď327 of the Companies Act, 2013, which provides special priority to workmen, apply during liquidation 
under IBC? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC Provisions 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.5(13): CIRP costs include ‚Äúany costs incurred by the RP in running the business of the CD as a going concern.‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.20: IRP/RP must endeavour to manage the CD as a going concern (no presumption that it actually is). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.36(4)(a)(iii): Excludes PF, gratuity, and pension funds from the liquidation estate. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.53: Waterfall: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CIRP + liquidation costs (paid in full first) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Workmen dues (pre-CIRP) with other unsecured creditors. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Companies Act, 2013 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.327(7): ss. 326‚Äď327 do not apply during liquidation under the IBC. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under CA 1956, workmen got special priority; IBC now displaces this. 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 1 ‚Äď When can wages during CIRP be treated as CIRP costs? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Court held strictly that two cumulative conditions must be proved: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CD was actually run as a going concern during CIRP, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The workmen/employees actually worked during such a period. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No presumption that CD is a going concern merely because s.20 imposes a duty on the RP. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Only those workers who actually worked while CD was run as a going concern ‚Üí CIRP costs (s.53(1)(a)). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Those who did not work or where the CD was not a going concern ‚Üí not CIRP costs. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Accepting the workers‚Äô argument (automatic inclusion of all wages) would produce ‚Äúabsurd 
consequences‚ÄĚ by giving priority to workers who did not contribute to CIRP operations. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 2 ‚Äď Priority of workmen dues under s.53 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Wages/salaries of workmen who actually worked during CIRP ‚Üí CIRP costs (s.53(1)(a)), paid in full before all 
others. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Pre-CIRP workmen dues ‚Üí fall under s.53(1)(b) & (c) along with other unsecured creditors. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
There is no special priority for 24-month wages under Companies Act because s.327(7) excludes that 
regime during IBC liquidation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Issue 3 ‚Äď PF, gratuity, pension funds 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.36(4)(a)(iii) expressly excludes these from the liquidation estate. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Workmen are entitled to PF/gratuity/pension directly from those funds. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Liquidators cannot touch these funds. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
They stand outside the s.53 waterfall. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Application to facts 
‚ěĘ‚Äč There were disputes on whether the Dahej yard/Mumbai office were functioning as going concerns during CIRP 
and whether workmen actually worked. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC directed: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Workmen must prove these facts before the liquidator. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The liquidator must adjudicate independently within 12 weeks. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Amount set aside must be used exclusively for adjudicated workmen dues. 
 
72 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Wages/salaries of workmen during CIRP can be included as CIRP costs only if 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD was run as a going concern, and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The concerned workmen actually worked during that period. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Such wages are paid first and in full under s.53(1)(a). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Pre-CIRP wages/salaries are not CIRP costs and fall under s.53(1)(b)/(c). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč PF, gratuity, pension fund dues are outside the liquidation estate (s.36(4)), not subject to s.53 waterfall, and must be paid 
from the respective funds. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Companies Act ss. 326‚Äď327 do not apply because of s.327(7).
cases: Lalit Kumar v Union of India
Dilip Jiwrajka v Union of India
BRS Ventures Investments Limited v SREI Infrastructure Finance Limited
key_principles: ‚Äč Multiple writ petitions across High Courts challenged the MCA Notification dated 15.11.2019, which was then transferred
‚Äč The notification operationalised Part III of the IBC only for personal guarantors to corporate debtors.
‚Äč The petitioners were individuals who had given personal guarantees for loans taken by companies they were connected with.
‚Äč After these companies entered CIRP, creditors invoked the guarantees and issued demand notices proposing Part III
‚Äč Contended that the notification exceeded Section 1(3), amounted to impermissible selective enforcement of Part III, violated
‚Äč They argued that Section 1(3) allows phased enforcement but not selective application to subclasses, that only the DRT can
‚Äč An instance of excessive delegation and ultra vires Section 1(3) of the IBC.
‚Äč Arbitrary and unconstitutional under Article 14 due to selective application only against personal guarantors.
‚Äč Contrary to the scheme of the IBC, which allegedly does not contemplate initiating Part III proceedings solely against
‚Äč Invalid because the Code does not permit personal guarantors to be treated differently or tried before the NCLT.
‚Äč Invalid because approval of a resolution plan under Part II automatically discharges guarantors.
‚Äč Section 1(3) of the IBC ‚Äď empowers the Central Government to notify different provisions of the Code on different dates.
‚Äč Section 2(e), (f), (g) (post-2018 amendment) ‚Äď creates separate categories:
‚Äč Section 2(e): personal guarantors to corporate debtors (distinct category).
‚Äč Section 2(f): partnership firms and partners.
‚Äč Section 2(g): individuals.
‚Äč Designates NCLT as the adjudicating authority for:
‚Äč Section 60(2) mandates that if CIRP of a corporate debtor is pending before NCLT, insolvency/bankruptcy of its
‚Äč Section 60(3) transfers pending proceedings against personal guarantors from DRT to NCLT.
‚Äč Section 60(4) grants NCLT all powers of DRT for Part III.
